evidence_basis: ICA s.17(f) statutorily mandates qualified custody of fund securities; fund assets are the property of the fund, a separate entity; bank custodians must keep fiduciary assets separate from bank assets (12 CFR 9.13(b)). Statutory, insolvency-effective without contract.

ICA s.18(f)(1) statutorily flattens the open-end capital structure (no senior securities; bank borrowings only at 300 percent asset coverage), making the holder's residual rank determinate by statute; holder status documented in the registration statement (485BPOS, Jan 2025). The statutorily flat capital structure satisfies D2 (rank determinate by statute).

UNCITRAL Model Law adopted as Bankruptcy Code Chapter 15 (Pub. L. 109-8, 2005), in force, with fund-relevant practice: Morning Mist v Krys (In re Fairfield Sentry), 714 F.3d 127 (2d Cir. 2013), affirming recognition of an offshore fund liquidation.

Product-class statutory layer: 1940 Act custody (17(f)), capital structure (18), redemption rights (22(e)); plus the money-market-fund regulatory layer (Rule 2a-7; Rule 22e-3 purpose-built orderly liquidation requiring irrevocable board approval and SEC notification).

Statutory lattice: s.17(f) custody mandate with express rule authority over earmarking, segregation, and hypothecation (17(f)(3)); s.18(f)(1) 300 percent coverage cap on borrowings; custodian reuse barred by 12 CFR 9.13. Pass under the substance-based D5 ladder (a custody-mandate-plus-leverage-cap lattice yields pass).

s.22(e) bars suspension beyond seven days except enumerated, disclosed circumstances or SEC order; the 2023 MMF reforms removed temporary gates from Rule 2a-7 (88 FR 51404); permanent suspension only via Rule 22e-3 liquidation; mandatory liquidity fees do not reach government funds. No officer power compels conversion into a different instrument.

Reserve Primary Fund: broke the buck 2008-09-16; court-ordered pro rata distribution recovered more than 98 cents on the dollar by January 2010 (SEC PR 2010-16; final figure 99.1 cents secondary-sourced, primary wishlisted). In-class, completed, timely by insolvency standards; the Reserve Primary recovery satisfies D7 timeliness.

Structurally inapplicable: a deposit is a bank liability (12 U.S.C. 1813(l)(1)); deposited funds are bank property; there is no customer asset to segregate. Claim-side substitutes (insurance, preference) are credited at product level per adopted ruling Q2(a).

National depositor preference fixes the rank by statute: deposit liabilities rank second, after receiver expenses and above all other general liabilities, subordinated debt, and shareholders. Statutory, uniform, contract-independent. Documentation prong assessed at product level per ruling Q4(a).

Banks are excluded from the Bankruptcy Code (11 U.S.C. 109(b)(2)) and from Chapter 15 (1501(c)(1)); the FDI Act receivership regime has no statutory foreign-recognition framework. Demonstrated coordinated parallel action exists (2023: FDIC resolved SVB while the Bank of England separately resolved SVB UK), which is practice without statutory recognition.

The strongest product-specific statutory layer in the matrix: dedicated receivership regime (12 U.S.C. 191; FDI Act), deposit insurance, depositor preference, bridge-bank tools (1821(n)), least-cost framework with systemic risk override (1823(c)(4)).

Structurally inapplicable: deploying depositor funds is the deposit-taking business model, not a re-pledge of customer assets; protection is claim-side (prudential regulation, insurance, preference), not asset-side. Rendered fail-by-inapplicability per ruling Q2(a).

Receiver powers statutorily enumerated and bounded: bridge transfer, purchase and assumption, insured payout 'as soon as possible'; uninsured balances convert to receivership claims by operation of pre-existing statute, not discretionary conversion. 2023 receiverships transferred all deposits whole.

Three completed in-class receiverships (2023): SVB and Signature (systemic risk exception made all depositors whole with next-business-day access); First Republic (whole-deposit P&A to JPMorgan). Insured record: no losses since 1933. Caveat: uninsured whole-making was discretionary, precedent not entitlement.

regime: US insured national bank, non-deposit stablecoin (FDI Act receivership) [SoFiUSD]
evidence_basis: No statute or binding regulation segregates a non-deposit stablecoin reserve at an insured full-service national bank; OCC Interpretive Letters 1174 and 1183 authorize bank stablecoin issuance with no segregation or titling mandate; 12 CFR 9.13 reaches only fiduciary-capacity assets and the SOFID Terms disclaim the capacity (no agent, broker, trustee, fiduciary, custodian) and negate any trust, bailment, escrow, custodial relationship, or holder ownership or security interest in Reserve Assets. Two published Deloitte and Touche LLP attestations (Report Dates 2026-03-31 and 2026-04-30; AICPA standards) cut the other way at the operational layer, describing the reserve held 100 percent in cash at the Federal Reserve through a BitGo Trust Company, Inc. segregated fiduciary account for the benefit of holders, legally segregated from both SoFi's and BitGo's corporate assets, so the contractual disclaimers and the attested FBO segregation are two-sided and unreconciled (the J3F pattern); but the attested custody is contractual and operational rather than a statute or binding regulation, which is what this dimension scores. The J2xD1 Fail* inapplicability premise (a deposit has no customer asset to segregate) does not carry; a plain fail on the statutory leg, with the two-sided contractual cure credited at the product level (it resolves the product verdict to Ambiguous rather than Fail).

regime: US insured national bank, non-deposit stablecoin (FDI Act receivership) [SoFiUSD]
evidence_basis: Unlike J3F's no-statute vacuum, 12 USC 1821(d)(11)(A) supplies a determinate, contract-independent receivership waterfall; on the issuer's own characterization that SOFID is not a deposit (12 USC 1813(l)), the holder occupies clause (iii), any other general or senior liability, structurally subordinated behind every insured and uninsured deposit liability, and the Terms document the rank adversely (claims may be treated as general unsecured obligations of the Issuer). Statute fixes the rank, but to the holder's disadvantage, and the depositor-preference leg that lifted J2xD2 to pass does not reach a non-deposit claim.

regime: US insured national bank, non-deposit stablecoin (FDI Act receivership) [SoFiUSD]
evidence_basis: Carries J2xD3 substantially, entity-level and claim-class-independent: the issuer is excluded from the Bankruptcy Code (11 USC 109(b)(2)) and Chapter 15 (1501(c)(1)) and the FDI Act receivership regime has no statutory foreign-recognition framework, but demonstrated coordinated parallel action exists (2023: FDIC resolved SVB while the Bank of England separately resolved SVB UK), practice without statutory recognition. Materially stronger than J3FxD3 fail: a real, modern coordination record.

regime: US insured national bank, non-deposit stablecoin (FDI Act receivership) [SoFiUSD]
evidence_basis: The FDI Act receivership machinery carries at entity level (dedicated regime, 12 USC 191; bridge-bank tools, 1821(n); least-cost framework with systemic-risk override, 1823(c)(4)), but the two legs that made J2xD4 the matrix's strongest product-specific statutory layer, deposit insurance and depositor preference, are expressly disclaimed for this product (SOFID is not a deposit and is not insured by the FDIC; holders have no pass-through insurance). Carries the entity-level leg, loses the deposit-specific legs.

regime: US insured national bank, non-deposit stablecoin (FDI Act receivership) [SoFiUSD]
evidence_basis: No operative legal constraint reaches the reserve: OCC IL 1174 imposes none; 12 CFR Part 9 does not attach absent the disclaimed fiduciary capacity; GENIUS Sec. 4(a)(2) (no pledge, rehypothecation, or reuse) is dormant and conditioned on a permitted-issuer status the issuer does not hold. The published Deloitte attestations describe the reserve held in a BitGo Trust Company, Inc. segregated fiduciary account for the benefit of holders (not deployed as the bank's own balance-sheet assets), and the SOFID Terms carry an anti-encumbrance covenant, but both are contractual and operational rather than a statutory or binding-regulatory prohibition, and the covenant is unsecured, liability-capped, and repudiable, so neither lifts the fail-grade absence under the D5 ladder; the J2xD5 Fail* inapplicability premise does not carry. Fires the Q1a cap (the contractual cure resolves the product verdict to Ambiguous, not Fail).

regime: US insured national bank, non-deposit stablecoin (FDI Act receivership) [SoFiUSD]
evidence_basis: Receiver powers are statutorily enumerated and bounded with a claims process and judicial review (1821(d)) and distribution is in money, so the bounded-powers leg of J2xD6 carries; short of pass because the receiver may disaffirm or repudiate any contract (1821(e)(1)) with liability limited to actual direct compensatory damages (1821(e)(3)(A)), converting the par-redemption undertaking into a fixed dividend claim, a power the issuer's own Risk Disclosure flags, and a substitute-issuer right operates without holder consent.

regime: US insured national bank, non-deposit stablecoin (FDI Act receivership) [SoFiUSD]
evidence_basis: Zero in-class precedent: no insured bank issuing a non-deposit stablecoin has entered receivership and the bank-issued-token holder cohort record is empty; but the adjacent class is real, modern, and completed, unlike J3FxD7's archaic record (hundreds of FDIA receiverships with documented claims and dividend machinery), with the caveat that the relevant claimant class (clause (iii) general creditors, subordinated below the entire deposit book) carries no recovery-fraction record. Fail defensible; partial on the modern-machinery strength.

GENIUS Sec. 10(c)(1) segregation and Sec. 11(e) estate exclusion are enacted but not effective (Sec. 20; no final rules as of 2026-06-11); the operative layer is trust-company structure plus the NYDFS updated custody guidance (2025-09-30), regulator guidance whose insolvency effectiveness is untested; structurally, Circle discloses (424(b)(4), 2025-08-15) that about 87 percent of USDC reserves sit in the Circle Reserve Fund (Rule 2a-7 government MMF, BNY-custodied) with the cash remainder in FBO-titled accounts at GSIBs, segregation-supporting titling whose insolvency effectiveness is exactly what the issuer's own risk factor concedes is judicially untested. Ruling Q3(a): score the operative regime; re-score trigger registered.

GENIUS Sec. 11(a) ratable holder priority and new 11 U.S.C. 507(e) are enacted but dormant and academically contested (internal-contradiction critique); pre-effectiveness, holders are unsecured creditors of a money transmitter or trust company, with Celsius (647 B.R. 631) illustrating recharacterization exposure of terms-based claims; Circle's own prospectus risk factor concedes courts have not considered reserve-asset treatment in a stablecoin issuer insolvency and that a court could find the reserves property of the bankruptcy estate, with automatic-stay delay even on the holder-favorable reading.

Chapter 15 in force with directly relevant crypto-asset practice: recognition of Three Arrows Capital's BVI liquidation as foreign main proceeding (order ECF No. 47, 2022-07-28, Case No. 22-10920 (MG) Bankr. S.D.N.Y.).

A product-class statutory insolvency layer exists by enacted statute (GENIUS Sec. 11, amending Bankruptcy Code 101/362/507/541/1109) but is not in force; today's holder-level protections are structural (trust charters, segregated accounts) and guidance-based, with regulator intervention powers. Flips to pass on the same evidence at the Sec. 20 date.

Binding regulatory prohibition, verified against primary text 2026-06-11: 23 NYCRR 200.9(c) bars a licensee from selling, transferring, assigning, lending, hypothecating, pledging, or otherwise using or encumbering assets held on behalf of another person except at that person's direction (customer-direction exception preserves pass under the adopted D5 ladder; the Singapore reg 18C(1)(c) parallel), and 200.9(b) imposes a same-type-and-amount coverage requirement; the NYDFS 2025 custody guidance bars use of customer assets as collateral; Circle's SEC-filed prospectus (424(b)(4), 2025-08-15) characterizes the MTL and BitLicense/Virtual Currency License regulations as prohibiting rehypothecation of reserve assets held for the benefit of holders. Reach caveat documented: whether 200.9 reaches an issuer's own reserve pool is the same characterization question as the Singapore reserve-side gap; graded consistently with J9xD5. GENIUS Sec. 4(a)(2) statutory prohibition remains dormant. Re-graded from partial: the binding regulatory prohibition basis is surfaced by the Circle prospectus.

Once effective, GENIUS channels redemption into court-supervised ratable distributions on a 14-day best-efforts clock (new 362(a)(9), (d)(5)); pre-effectiveness, chapter 11 practice converted holder claims into different instruments (Celsius: crypto plus Ionic Digital equity; Voyager: USDC substitution), bounded only by general plan-confirmation law.

No stablecoin issuer has failed; adjacent-class precedent is completed but slow, partial, and litigated: Celsius custody settlement returned 72.5 percent (order ECF 2291, 2023-03-21); Voyager initial recovery approximately 35 percent (confirmation ECF 1159, 2023-03-08); Celsius emerged 2024-01-31 distributing over $3B.

regime: OCC uninsured national trust bank (J3 federal layer only) [USD1, PYUSD]
evidence_basis: Under the federal-layer (J3F) derivation rule, the dormant-GENIUS leg scores in the issuer's own right (GENIUS Sec. 10(c) segregation enacted but not effective; Sec. 20; no final rules as of 2026-06-12). The operative layer is the trust-bank structure: the issuer's terms state the fiat reserve is held in accounts segregated from the issuer's own assets, and the Crowe LLP examination-level attestation series describes a segregated qualified trust for the benefit of USD1 holders, while the same terms disclaim any holder security or property interest in the reserve and the OCC conditional-approval letter records the Reserve Assets on the bank's own balance sheet in granting capital-treatment relief under 12 CFR 3.1(d). Segregation-supporting titling whose insolvency effectiveness is unreconciled and untested; no published analysis addresses holder treatment in a 12 CFR Part 51 receivership. PYUSD joined the row 2026-06-12 on parallel facts with the segregation-supporting layer stronger at terms level: the Paxos US Dollar-Backed Stablecoin Terms (Last Modified 2025-12-12) covenant that reserve dollars will always be held in segregated accounts apart from corporate funds for the benefit of holders, carry an in-terms bankruptcy-remote representation, and contain no property-interest disclaimer, while the Paxos OCC letter (Charter 25379) records the same unreconciled recital pair (Reserve Assets held in custody and segregated from proprietary assets; held on PTC's balance sheet under 12 CFR 3.1(d) relief); equally untested in a Part 51 receivership.

regime: OCC uninsured national trust bank (J3 federal layer only) [USD1, PYUSD]
evidence_basis: Under the federal-layer (J3F) derivation rule, GENIUS Sec. 11 ratable holder priority is enacted but dormant; pre-effectiveness no statute fixes a USD1 holder's rank in an OCC receivership of an uninsured national trust bank (national depositor preference, 12 U.S.C. 1821(d)(11), does not reach a no-deposit institution), and claim rank turns on the unresolved channel question between an in-kind fiduciary or custodial claim and a general unsecured money claim (12 CFR 51.7(b), 51.8(b)): the FBO trust titling is unreconciled with the terms' no-property-interest clauses and no published receivership-treatment analysis exists. The same unresolved channel question holds for PYUSD: the Paxos terms affirmatively record holder interests (no disclaimer), strengthening the in-kind fiduciary or custodial-claim reading, but no statute fixes rank and no published receivership-treatment analysis exists for either issuer.

regime: OCC uninsured national trust bank (J3 federal layer only) [USD1, PYUSD]
evidence_basis: As in J2, the issuer is excluded from the Bankruptcy Code as a bank (11 U.S.C. 109(b)(2)) and from Chapter 15 (1501(c)(1)); the operative forum is OCC receivership under the National Bank Act (12 U.S.C. 191-200; 12 CFR Part 51), which contains no foreign-recognition or coordination provision (the 2016 final rule and its preamble contain zero foreign, cross-border, or comity references, and the preamble records no court opinion finding an uninsured national bank Code-eligible). The leg that lifted J2 to PARTIAL is absent: the regime records no coordination practice of any kind, the OCC having appointed no uninsured-bank receiver since 1933. Inbound Chapter 15 recognition of foreign affiliate proceedings remains available in the jurisdiction but does not reach the holder's claim against the bank, which resolves only in the domestic ratable-distribution process (12 U.S.C. 194).

regime: OCC uninsured national trust bank (J3 federal layer only) [USD1, PYUSD]
evidence_basis: Under the federal-layer (J3F) derivation rule, a product-class statutory insolvency layer exists by enacted statute (GENIUS Sec. 10(c) segregation; Sec. 11 holder priority) but is not in force; the interim structural layer is the charter stack itself: OCC-supervised uninsured national trust bank (Charters 25366 and 25379), conditional-approval condition requiring conformance of its stablecoin activities with the GENIUS Act and any implementing regulations (verbatim in both letters; the Paxos Condition 2 verified 2026-06-12), and a dedicated receivership regime at 12 CFR Part 51. Flips toward pass on the same evidence at the Sec. 20 date; the ruling-3 re-score trigger extends to this row.

regime: OCC uninsured national trust bank (J3 federal layer only) [USD1, PYUSD]
evidence_basis: Under the federal-layer (J3F) derivation rule, no verified operative rehypothecation constraint reaches the issuer. 23 NYCRR 200.9 and the NYDFS guidance layer are entity-scoped to NYDFS-supervised issuers and do not reach this charter stack (the NYDFS-chartered BitGo affiliate is not the issuer and holds no attested reserve custody); GENIUS Sec. 4(a)(2) is dormant; the OCC fiduciary-account rules (12 CFR Part 9) are of unanalyzed applicability to the reserve trust; the contractual FBO titling is unreconciled with the terms' no-property-interest clauses. A fail-grade absence of constraint, not an affirmatively permissive regime; fires the Q1a cap on the product verdict. The same absence holds for PYUSD post-conversion (NYDFS anchors entity-scoped away; consummation evidenced by the OCC active trust-bank list of 2026-05-31; GENIUS Sec. 4(a)(2) dormant; 12 CFR Part 9 of unanalyzed applicability, posed as Question 165 in the OCC's own GENIUS NPRM; no reserve-management condition in the Paxos letter), with the contract layer stronger than BitGo's but carrying no express no-pledge or no-lend covenant (search-negative) and contemplating repo deployment, so contractual-only and unable to lift the fail-grade absence.

regime: OCC uninsured national trust bank (J3 federal layer only) [USD1, PYUSD]
evidence_basis: Distribution form in the operative forum is statutorily money and ratable: 12 U.S.C. 194 requires ratable dividends 'of the money' on claims proved or adjudicated, remainder to shareholders; 12 CFR 51.8(a) implements ratable dividends per the 51.5 priority, and the rule's preamble records the OCC reading that the ratable-payment statute supports no distribution distinctions between customer and general creditor claimants (81 FR 92594). No bridge, plan, or exchange machinery exists, so the chapter 11 practice that converted holder claims into different instruments (Celsius; Voyager) is structurally unavailable in the forum the issuer's own approval letter names (12 CFR 51). Short of pass on four verified legs: dividend timing is 'periodically, at the discretion of the OCC' (51.8(c)) with no statutory clock; the OCC has not appointed an uninsured-bank receiver since shortly after the FDIC's creation (81 FR 92594); the holder's channel is unresolved between an in-kind direct claim for fiduciary or custodial assets (51.7(b); 51.8(b), turning on book designation the D1/D4 balance-sheet recital and no-property-interest terms leave unreconciled) and a general unsecured money claim; and the GENIUS overlay is dormant, with Sec. 11(a) ratable holder priority (12 USC 5910) reaching 'any insolvency proceeding ... under Federal or State law' only at the Sec. 20 date, the 14-day channel (new 362(a)(9), (d)(5)) a title 11 amendment that does not reach this non-Code forum, and Sec. 11(g) naming the FDIC, NCUA, and State regulators but not the OCC.

regime: OCC uninsured national trust bank (J3 federal layer only) [USD1, PYUSD]
evidence_basis: Zero modern in-forum precedent: the Part 51 rulemaking preamble records that the OCC 'has not appointed a receiver for an uninsured bank since shortly after the Congress established the FDIC in response to the banking panics of 1930-1933' (81 FR 92594, 2016-12-20; rule effective 2017-01-19), that weakened trust banks have historically been rehabilitated or resolved without a receiver, and no Part 51 receivership is recorded as of 2026-06-12; the BitGo approval letter (2025-12-12) answers resolvability comments with capability language ('The OCC has the capability to resolve an uninsured national bank'), not precedent. The only in-forum record is pre-FDIC: 2,762 National Bank Act receiverships, nine decades remote, depository-bank, with no recovery-fraction record in the preamble; for era context, the FDIC documents depositor losses across all failed banks (1865-1934, state and national, not the national-bank receivership cohort alone) as equivalent to a one-third of one percent annual deposit assessment. The adjacent-class chapter 11 record grounding J3's partial (Celsius, Voyager) does not transfer because banks are excluded debtors (11 USC 109(b)(2), per J2xD3), and the FDI Act record (J2xD7) belongs to a different receiver and statute. Ratable-dividend machinery exists on paper (12 USC 194; 12 CFR 51.8, with the 51.8(b) fiduciary-asset carve-out) but has never produced a completed modern case; graded fail in parallel with J8xD7 (operative regime unused; fallback record archaic).

Delaware statutory entity-shielding (the LP is a separate legal entity) places fund assets outside the manager's and GP's estates, but this is not customer-asset segregation in the licensing-regime sense; the note sub-posture's collateral IS estate property reached through a perfected security interest (priority, not segregation); separate-accounts arrangements are contractual.

Both sub-postures statutorily fixed and documented, at opposite ends: LP equity ranks last (6 Del. C. 17-804(a); 11 U.S.C. 726(a)(6); absolute priority 1129(b)(2)(B)(ii); 510(b) blocks upgrading), documented in OUSG materials; the USDY note holds first-priority perfected rank (9-322 first-to-file; lien retention in cram-down 1129(b)(2)(A)), documented with security agent (Ankura) disclosure (the OGM-primary title).
Chapter 15 in force with deep practice, including the exact configuration the migrated USDY issuer would present (BVI company, US assets and agents): Three Arrows recognition (ECF 47, 2022-07-28).

No statute provides insolvency protections specific to tokenized notes or tokenized LP funds; holder-level protections (bankruptcy-remote SPV, security agent empowered to liquidate on default) are structural and contractual. The one product-specific statutory layer (GENIUS payment-stablecoin priority in 507) does not reach yield-bearing notes or LP interests.

Sub-posture split (adopted ruling Q6(a)): the secured-note sub-posture earns partial because UCC 9-322 first-to-file priority means later re-pledging cannot prime the noteholders' perfected lien (statutory harm backstop), while the act-of-repledge constraint is contractual and the security agreement non-public; the LP sub-posture fails (6 Del. C. 9-207(c)(3) affirmatively permits repledge by a secured party; constraints contractual only). Product derivations use the applicable sub-posture.

Chapter 11 cram-down can compel instrument exchange without consent, but statutorily bounded (best-interests floor 1129(a)(7); lien retention or indubitable equivalent 1129(b)(2)(A); absolute priority). Product triggers are pre-agreed and disclosed (USDY Event of Default mandating collateral liquidation). Compulsion outside pre-agreed triggers keeps this at partial.

No tokenized-note or tokenized-LP insolvency has completed; adjacent precedent is deep (Article 9 enforcement is among the most practiced US commercial-law tracks; Delaware Chancery receivership machinery), and the crypto-fund channel (Three Arrows) was offshore-led, slow, and litigated.

Recognised funds must at all times have a functionally independent custodian (Mutual Funds Regulations reg 7, FSC-waivable) and trust-held assets are excluded from a BVI company's insolvency estate (Insolvency Act s.207(4)), but there is no product-specific statutory trust over fund assets; insolvency effectiveness against a custodian or manager estate rests on general trust and property law.

Statute fixes the waterfall (s.207) and subordinates member-character claims including redemption proceeds (s.197); BVI Court of Appeal authority places redeemed-but-unpaid members as deferred creditors (Somers Dublin v Monarch Pointe); Privy Council fixed NAV-certificate finality (Fairfield Sentry v Migani [2014] UKPC 9). Rank determinable only via the redemption-creditor characterization analysis the rubric names as partial.

The Model Law was enacted as Insolvency Act 2003 Part XVIII but never brought into force (Net International Property line; uniform practitioner confirmation through 2026); in-force Part XIX orders-in-aid are designation-gated (US designated; 24 jurisdictions added effective 2024-09-18, 33 total); common-law recognition survives without common-law assistance. Outbound coordination is real but asymmetric (US recognized the 3AC BVI liquidation under Chapter 15).

A product-specific regulatory layer exists (SIBA fund recognition; mandatory functionaries) and the FSC has statutory standing to seek liquidation of a regulated person (Insolvency Act s.162), but the FSC must apply to COURT for an administrator (FSCA s.39, verified against the Cayman contrast); holder-level protections are structural and contractual, and the regime's own prescribed warning states professional funds are not subject to FSC supervision.

Full-text search of the consolidated Insolvency Act, SIBA, and Mutual Funds Regulations found no statutory or regulatory prohibition or quantitative limit on re-pledging fund assets; the custody requirement is structural and FSC-waivable; any constraint sits in the non-public BNY Mellon custody agreement and cannot be verified.

Liquidation default is money dividends through the s.207 waterfall with court sanction for significant liquidator actions (s.186(5); evidenced by the sanctioned 3AC US$100M interim distribution); suspension and gating powers are constitutional-document-based, upheld with NAV-certificate finality in Fairfield Sentry: disclosed and bounded.

Directly comparable precedents exist but recovery is slow and litigated: Three Arrows (liquidation 2022-06-27; first court-sanctioned interim distribution, up to US$100M against claims above US$3B, only on 2024-03-28; still running) and Fairfield Sentry's decade-plus clawback arc to the Privy Council.

Fund-vs-manager segregation rests on corporate separateness plus CIMA's binding Segregation of Assets Rule (5.2, 5.5), untested in a service-provider insolvency and carrying a disclosed-consent reuse carve-out (5.6.5); the regime's strong statutory partitioning (Part 14 SPC segregation) does not apply because the fund is not an SPC (Companies Act s.215 naming inference; register confirmation reCAPTCHA-blocked, flagged).

Waterfall fixed by statute (Companies Act s.37(7)(b), s.49(g)) and settled apex authority (Pearson v Primeo [2017] UKPC 19: redemption effective on surrender; redemption creditors deferred behind ordinary creditors, ahead of members), but locating a holder requires exactly the multi-step characterization the rubric's partial prong names, and the redemption window is clawback-exposed (SEB v Conway [2019] UKPC 36).

Cayman has not adopted the Model Law (verified against the UNCITRAL status list); Part 17 of the Companies Act (ss.240-243) is an in-force, inbound-only, discretionary ancillary-orders regime, with real product-class practice (Herald realised approximately USD 259M from the US BLMIS estate). High partial: statutory assistance plus practice, no recognition regime.

CIMA holds a DIRECT administrative power to appoint an adviser or a controller who displaces the operator and may terminate the business (Mutual Funds Act s.30(3)(d)-(e), (7), (8)), with no court order required: the statutory layer an undifferentiated offshore basis erased, and one the BVI does not replicate. Under the rubric, controllership is the partial prong's named example; holder-level protections beyond it are structural.

A binding regulatory constraint above contract exists (CIMA Rule 5.5: no service provider may use the portfolio to finance its own operations), but Rule 5.6.5 permits transfer and reuse with fund consent on pre-investment disclosure of the maximum permitted level: a disclosure-conditioned constraint, not a hard limit, demoted to partial under the adopted D5 ladder (fund-level consent carve-out).

No statute empowers unilateral conversion: compulsory restructuring requires court-sanctioned compromise with class voting (restructuring-officer gateway s.91B, in force); suspension and gating are articles-based, disclosed and pre-agreed; s.37(7) proviso (ii) can statutorily defeat redemption enforcement where lawful distribution was impossible.

Multiple completed or far-advanced in-class fund insolvencies with documented recovery, all slow and heavily litigated: Weavering (2009 liquidation; preference recoveries settled at the Privy Council 2019) and Herald (2013 wind-up; USD 259M first realisation 2015; priority litigation through [2017] UKPC 19 and [2020] UKPC 3).

Statutory estate carve-out: client digital assets held in custody are 'not property or digital assets of the licensed undertaking' and 'not subject to the claims of creditors' (DABA 2018 s.18(4)); the Custody of Client Assets Rules 2025 (in force 2025-02-12) add beneficial-ownership holding (r 9) and insolvency-triggered pari passu pooling (rr 11-12). Reach caveat carried at product level: the regime covers what CIBL custodies; the share-register linkage question routes to the USYC dual-layer analysis.

Client assets sit outside the estate and distribute pari passu among clients; residual shortfall claims rank as unsecured under Companies Act 1981 ss.225/236 with no customer preference class. For a tokenholder, rank requires multi-step characterization (client vs counterparty; shareholder vs claimant against the issuer), and product materials do not document rank.

Bermuda has not adopted the Model Law (verified) and has no statutory recognition regime; recognition runs through common-law comity, with strong recent digital-asset practice: BlockFi International (JPLs; court-approved cross-border protocol with the US Chapter 11 estate; recognition of the confirmed US plan; winding-up order 2023-11-10).

Product-specific statutory protections layered on general insolvency law: statutory estate carve-out (s.18(4)), 100 percent coverage duty (s.18(3)), Authority winding-up petition power (s.25), client-interest directions (s.28), insolvency-likelihood reporting duty (s.20), plus the binding default-and-pooling distribution regime keyed to insolvency commencement in any jurisdiction.

Statutory quantitative constraint: the custodian must maintain sufficient digital assets of each type to meet client obligations at all times (DABA s.18(3)); the Custody Rules bar mixing and cross-client use (r 15(1)); the BMA Custody Code's express no-rehypothecation language sits at code level above the statutory floor. Pass under the adopted D5 ladder.

On default the Custody Rules substitute a statutory pari passu pooling distribution for the undertaking's powers (disclosed, bounded shortfall mutualisation), and client assets are beyond liquidator reach (s.18(4)); but Bermuda practice (BlockFi) channels holder claims into a foreign plan's mechanics via recognition, and schemes can bind dissenting holders with court sanction.

A BMA digital-asset licensee has failed and been wound up: BlockFi International (JPLs Nov 2022; winding-up 2023-11-10; US-plan recognition; distributions from January 2024), roughly 14 months to first distributions through the coordinated US Chapter 11; the product class (crypto lending) is adjacent to a token-issuer, and no token-issuer-class DABA failure exists.

Evidence-confirmed: the full texts of LEAD (Decreto 643/2023), REOPME, and the DASP Regulation (official CNAD copies) contain no segregation, trust, or customer-property provision (zero hits for segregacion, patrimonio separado, quiebra, insolvencia across 48 DASP articles); REOPME Art. 14 permits offshore custody on a notify-only basis; quiebra law sweeps all debtor assets into the masa (Codigo de Comercio Art. 503); the anchor product's terms affirmatively disclaim segregation.

No Salvadoran statute or case law fixes where a stablecoin holder ranks in the quiebra waterfall; most holders have no claim against any entity (redemption is contractually limited to verified customers); the fail branch governs the holder population at large. Adopted Q7 synthesis: the cell documents that a verified customer's redemption claim IS determinable as a general unsecured claim after multi-jurisdiction analysis (El Salvador issuer; BVI-law arbitration ToS; US-custodied reserves).

El Salvador has not adopted the Model Law (verified against the UNCITRAL status page) and no insolvency-specific recognition regime was found in LEAD, REOPME, or the commercial codes; no coordination practice exists. Adopted Q7 synthesis: general exequatur under the Codigo Procesal Civil y Mercantil was NOT verified and is not credited; an upgrade-to-partial trigger is registered if the recognition provisions verify at acquisition.

Regulator intervention powers specific to the product class exist by statute and regulation: CNAD may suspend operations and revoke registrations (LEAD Art. 9(d)), cancel a stablecoin authorization on redemption failure or reserve insufficiency (REOPME Art. 17), impose the trading-ban list (Art. 18), and require orderly-liquidation contingency plans (LEAD Art. 21(p)); holder-level protections remain absent. Attachment caveat: USDT is admitted only via the Art. 7 No Objection route, not as a registered issuance; whether REOPME issuer obligations attach to USDT is untested.

Evidence-confirmed: no statutory or regulatory prohibition or limit on pledging or re-using reserve or customer assets anywhere in the regime; eligible reserves include secured loans; CNAD reserves the right to exempt any issuer from any reserve requirement (REOPME Art. 4(d)); the product terms state reserves may include affiliate loan receivables.

The quiebra regime divests the debtor with no statutory bounds protecting holders against conversion or suspension; REOPME defines redemption only as conversion within a prudent time with no holder-protective timeline; the issuer's contractual power to suspend redemptions at its sole discretion is a broad undisclosed-trigger power.

No completed insolvency of a digital-asset issuer or DASP under the CNAD regime (operative since 2023); the fallback is the little-used 1970 Codigo de Comercio quiebra framework with no comparable-product precedent.

The custody-side statute is strong and in force: PSA s.23(7) and PSR reg 18B(3) place customer money and DPT assets in trust accounts that 'cannot be used for the payment of the debts of the licensee' (in force 2024-10-04; judicially untested); but the issuer-reserve side (SCS framework: reserves in segregated accounts on trust) is finalized policy awaiting legislation (MAS, Nov 2025: 'will be preparing draft legislation'), so USDG reserve segregation rests on MPI licence conditions and the Paxos/DBS trust structure. Re-score trigger registered.

Custodied customer assets sit outside the estate by operation of reg 18B(3)/s.23(7) (property, not priority); but a stablecoin holder's redemption claim against the issuer requires multi-step characterization and no statute yet fixes SCS holders' rank; USDG holder rights are presently contract and structure.

Model Law has force of law via IRDA s.252 and the Third Schedule (in force 2020-07-30), with direct crypto practice: Re Zipmex [2022] SGHC 196 (group moratoria; Singapore COMI for foreign entities) and Re Quoine [2025] SGHC(I) 5 (SICC recognition of the FTX-group US Chapter 11 as foreign main proceeding). The cleanest non-US D3 profile in the matrix.

An in-force product-class-adjacent statutory layer exists (PSA s.21A plus PSR Division 2A: DPT customer-asset trust with criminal offences), but the stablecoin-issuance-specific protections (100 percent par backing; five-business-day par redemption; segregated trust custody of reserves) are finalized regulatory policy awaiting legislation.

Regulatory prohibition anchored in the safeguarding regime: the licensee must not transfer any right, interest, benefit or title in trust-account customer assets to itself or anyone, except on the customer's specific prior written instructions (reg 18C(1)(c), (3)); commingling banned (18C(1)(b)); criminal offence (reg 18J(2), fine to $250,000). All sub-provisions independently re-verified 2026-06-11. Customer-level consent exception preserves pass under the adopted D5 ladder.

Insolvency law permits gating in practice: IRDA s.64 moratoria suspended customer withdrawals at Zipmex for months, and schemes of arrangement can restructure claims into different instruments, both court-supervised and statutorily bounded but not pre-agreed product triggers; statutory trust assets sit outside the estate, bounding officer reach.

Real adjacent-class proceedings with functioning machinery but slow recovery: Hodlnaut reached its first interim dividend only in February 2026, about 3.5 years after failure; Zipmex restructured under moratoria; Quoine creditors recovered through the recognized FTX plan. No completed stablecoin-issuer insolvency.

No issuing, managing, or custodial entity exists whose estate collateral could be segregated from (Maker Foundation dissolved 2021; the Dai Foundation holds trademarks only). Functional analog, documented not graded: collateral sits in protocol adapter contracts outside any firm's balance sheet.

A DAI holder has no claim against any entity; no waterfall rank exists to fix. Analog: the protocol hardcodes a de facto recovery ordering (liquidation auctions; on Global Settlement, vault owners take excess collateral first, DAI holders share pro rata at a fixed price). Code, not law; governance-mutable.
No debtor entity, no jurisdiction of incorporation, no foreign proceeding to recognize; the Model Law framework presupposes a debtor with an estate and a forum.

No statutory layer applies in any insolvency-adjacent sense, and there is no general corporate insolvency backstop because there is no entity; protections are structural (overcollateralization, liquidation auctions, settlement module), below even the contractual tier.

No statute, regulation, or custody agreement constrains re-pledging because no custodian holds the collateral. Analog: vault collateral is locked and not re-pledged by design, but the constraint is parameter-deep; governance reallocated de facto backing toward PSM stablecoin holdings (the March 2023 contagion channel).

No insolvency officer exists and no statute bounds any conversion power. Analog, fully coded and disclosed: Emergency Shutdown is a real forced-conversion mechanism (MKR depositors can trigger End.cage(), converting every holder's position into an optional pro-rata collateral claim at a fixed price). Scored fail-by-inapplicability per ruling Q2(a); the analog is documented, not graded.

No insolvency has occurred or could occur in any legal regime. The protocol-level precedent is real and two-sided: March 2020 zero-bid auctions took $8.32M of collateral and the system recapitalized via MKR dilution with DAI holders kept whole and vault owners voted 0 percent compensation; March 2023 PSM contagion (88 cents) recovered in days. No legal forum supplied any recovery.

In force (directly applicable Regulation; stablecoin titles from 2024-06-30): Art 36(2) legally segregates the reserve of assets from the issuers' estate so that creditors of the issuer have no recourse to the reserve, in particular on insolvency; Art 36(3) adds operational segregation. Statutory and insolvency-effective. Refuter: the clause is qualified 'in accordance with applicable law', deferring mechanics to member-state insolvency, but as a Regulation (not a Directive) Art 36(2) binds uniformly without transposition. Newly scored 2026-06-18 against eu_mica NOTES; one adversarial refuter. D1 ladder: rung 1 (statutory estate-exclusion).

Holders are satisfied from the ringfenced reserve ahead of the issuer's general creditors: Art 36(7) requires the reserve's aggregate value to at least equal aggregate holder claims and Art 36(2) bars creditor recourse; EMT holders carry a statutory claim (Art 49(2)). Refuter: a residual shortfall claim against the general estate is not MiCA-rank-fixed (national law), though the 1:1 backing and reserve-value duty minimise shortfall. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

The EU Insolvency Regulation (recast) 2015/848 gives automatic intra-EU recognition of a member-state main proceeding; extra-EU recognition is national, several member states having adopted the UNCITRAL Model Law. Refuter: the EIR is intra-EU only and extra-EU recognition varies by member state, short of a uniform inbound regime. Newly scored 2026-06-18 (regime-framework basis; primary-text re-verification flagged); one refuter.

MiCA is a comprehensive product-specific statutory layer, in force: own funds (Art 35), reserve and segregation (Art 36), reserve custody (Art 37-38), recovery plan (Art 46), orderly redemption plan (Art 47). Refuter: insolvency-distribution detail is deferred to national insolvency law and EBA Level-2 RTS. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

Under the substance-based D5 ladder: the reserve is segregated from the issuer's estate (Art 36(2)) and held by a custodian (Art 37-38), so the issuer cannot reuse or re-pledge it for its own account. Refuter: MiCA has no single express no-rehypothecation article (unlike 23 NYCRR 200.9); the constraint is achieved through the segregation-plus-custody architecture. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

The orderly-redemption plan (Art 47) and recovery plan (Art 46) bound the wind-down and holders redeem against the segregated reserve (Art 39); but the estate proceeding runs under national insolvency law, which could restructure a residual claim. Refuter: MiCA's redemption-plan discipline narrows officer reach but does not displace national restructuring law. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

No completed insolvency of a MiCA-authorised issuer (stablecoin titles in force only from 2024-06-30); no in-class recovery record. Refuter: the EMD2/PSD2 safeguarding regime MiCA builds on has adjacent e-money-failure practice, but no MiCA-stablecoin-issuer-class record; graded fail in parallel with the other new in-force regimes (J13, J14). Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UK (FCA CP25/14 issuer-tier + BoE systemic overlay) [GBP proposed]
evidence_basis: The FCA proposed statutory trust (CP25/14 3.86: backing ringfenced from creditor claims on issuer failure) is DRAFT (consultation closed 2025-07-31; final rules expected 2026); pre-enactment, holder protection rests on contractual trust structuring under general UK trust and property law. Refuter: the strong statutory trust is proposed, not in force; scored on the operative state per the dormancy treatment (J3 precedent), FCA final rules the re-score trigger. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UK (FCA CP25/14 issuer-tier + BoE systemic overlay) [GBP proposed]
evidence_basis: Pre-enactment a UK stablecoin holder is a general unsecured creditor of the issuer with no statutory priority; the draft regime would ringfence the backing on a statutory trust, fixing recovery from the trust assets. Refuter: the priority is proposed, not enacted; re-score trigger at FCA final rules. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UK (FCA CP25/14 issuer-tier + BoE systemic overlay) [GBP proposed]
evidence_basis: The UK has the Cross-Border Insolvency Regulations 2006 enacting the UNCITRAL Model Law, in force with deep practice, plus s.426 Insolvency Act 1986 assistance and common-law recognition. Refuter: none material; the UK cross-border regime is among the most robust, independent of the draft stablecoin rules. Newly scored 2026-06-18 (regime-framework basis); one refuter.

regime: UK (FCA CP25/14 issuer-tier + BoE systemic overlay) [GBP proposed]
evidence_basis: The product-specific layer is DRAFT and two-tier: the FCA issuer-tier rules (CP25/14) and the Bank of England systemic-tier regime (a proposed special-administration regime, 40 percent unremunerated BoE deposits and up to 60 percent UK gilts); the operative layer today is general FSMA and insolvency law. Refuter: the FCA/BoE regime is proposed; re-score trigger at the FCA final rules and the BoE Codes of Practice (2026). Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UK (FCA CP25/14 issuer-tier + BoE systemic overlay) [GBP proposed]
evidence_basis: The proposed FCA safeguarding regime (statutory trust; backing not usable for the issuer) is draft; pre-enactment, no-reuse constraints rest on the contractual trust. Refuter: proposed, not enacted; re-score trigger at FCA final rules. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UK (FCA CP25/14 issuer-tier + BoE systemic overlay) [GBP proposed]
evidence_basis: The BoE systemic-tier proposes a bespoke special-administration regime for systemic stablecoins; the FCA issuer-tier defers to general insolvency; both are draft. Refuter: the special-administration regime is proposed; pre-enactment, officer reach is governed by general UK insolvency and restructuring law. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UK (FCA CP25/14 issuer-tier + BoE systemic overlay) [GBP proposed]
evidence_basis: No UK stablecoin-issuer insolvency under the (draft) regime; no in-class recovery record. Refuter: the UK has general insolvency practice and an e-money-safeguarding record, but no stablecoin-issuer-class record under the new regime; graded fail. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: Hong Kong (Stablecoins Ordinance Cap. 656 / HKMA) [HKD FRS]
evidence_basis: The primary statute mandates a statutory ringfence (no trust): Schedule 2 section 5(1) segregates each reserve pool, and section 5(4) requires it 'is adequately protected against claims by other creditors of the licensee in all circumstances' and 'is kept separate'. The HKMA Supervisory Guideline separately requires an issuer-level effective trust over the reserve assets (paragraph 2.5.2: 'held for and on behalf of specified stablecoin holders', via 'the appointment of an independent trustee or a declaration of trust', backed by an independent legal opinion). Mechanism: statutory ringfence (Cap.656) plus a supervisory-mandated effective trust (the Guideline), D1 ladder rung 2 (statutory ringfence) reinforced toward rung 1. Refuter: the trust leg is framed with 'should' (a supervisory expectation per Guideline paragraph 1.2), not primary-statute text, and the statute itself uses no trust word in Schedule 2; the ringfence outcome is statutory, the trust is the supervisory implementation. Re-bound 2026-06-18 from the secondary Explanatory Note to the Cap.656 primary statute plus the Supervisory Guideline; prior unacquired-primary-text caveat discharged.

regime: Hong Kong (Stablecoins Ordinance Cap. 656 / HKMA) [HKD FRS]
evidence_basis: On issuer insolvency the holder's redemption is statutory and exercisable: Schedule 2 section 6(4) gives each holder, exercisable in the event of the licensee's insolvency, 'a right to direct the disposal of the specified reserve assets pool for the purpose of redeeming all the outstanding specified stablecoins of that type on a pro rata basis' plus 'a right to claim against the licensee for any shortfall'. Section 5(4)(a) ringfences that pool ahead of the licensee's other creditors, and the Guideline reinforces it by holding the reserve on an effective trust for and on behalf of holders (paragraph 2.5.2). Refuter: the residual shortfall claim under section 6(4)(b) is a claim against the licensee, a general unsecured claim in the liquidation; only the reserve-pool recovery is priority-fixed by section 5(4)(a) plus section 6(4)(a). Re-bound 2026-06-18 from the Explanatory Note to the Cap.656 primary statute (section 6(4)) plus the Supervisory Guideline trust leg.

regime: Hong Kong (Stablecoins Ordinance Cap. 656 / HKMA) [HKD FRS]
evidence_basis: Hong Kong has not adopted the UNCITRAL Model Law; cross-border recognition runs through common-law comity, where Hong Kong has substantial modern practice. Refuter: no statutory recognition or coordination regime; recognition is discretionary common-law, parallel to Cayman (J6) and Bermuda (J7). Newly scored 2026-06-18 (regime-framework basis); one refuter.

regime: Hong Kong (Stablecoins Ordinance Cap. 656 / HKMA) [HKD FRS]
evidence_basis: The Stablecoins Ordinance is a comprehensive product-specific statutory layer, in force (commenced 2025-08-01): Schedule 2 section 5 (reserve segregation, full at-par backing, independent attestation and audit), section 6 (redemption-at-par and the insolvency-exercisable rights), and section 16(2) (the licensee must ensure 'an orderly wind-down of its licensed stablecoin activities could be implemented'), plus the regulator-side statutory-manager resolution machinery at section 80 (the Monetary Authority 'may appoint a person to be a statutory manager of a licensee'). The Supervisory Guideline supplies the distribution detail (business exit plan, paragraph 6.8.17). Refuter: the detailed insolvency-distribution procedures sit in the Guideline (now acquired), framed with 'should' as supervisory expectations rather than statutory text. Re-bound 2026-06-18 from the Explanatory Note to the Cap.656 primary statute (sections 5, 6, 16, 80) plus the Supervisory Guideline.

regime: Hong Kong (Stablecoins Ordinance Cap. 656 / HKMA) [HKD FRS]
evidence_basis: The primary statute carries no express rehypothecation prohibition, so the no-reuse basis re-anchors to the Supervisory Guideline. The effective trust holds the reserve assets 'for and on behalf of specified stablecoin holders' (paragraph 2.5.2), precluding issuer reuse for its own account; only excess assets above the licensee's internal target may transfer to the licensee's own account (paragraph 2.5.3 excess-only transfer), so the par-backing reserve is not transferable to the issuer; qualified custodians safekeep the reserve, 'A custodian should be a licensed bank, or other asset custodian under an arrangement which is acceptable to the HKMA' (paragraph 2.5.4); and the custodial arrangement must not compromise full backing (paragraph 2.2.1). Pass under the substance-based D5 ladder via the trust-plus-excess-only-plus-qualified-custody-plus-full-backing lattice. Refuter: there is no single express no-rehypothecation article, and the no-reuse outcome is a construction from the Guideline framed with 'should' (a supervisory expectation per paragraph 1.2); the primary statute's nearest text is the section 5(5) high-quality-high-liquidity-minimal-risk standard, not a reuse prohibition. Re-bound 2026-06-18 from the Explanatory Note to the Supervisory Guideline (paragraphs 2.5.2, 2.5.3, 2.5.4, 2.2.1); prior unacquired-Guideline caveat discharged.

regime: Hong Kong (Stablecoins Ordinance Cap. 656 / HKMA) [HKD FRS]
evidence_basis: The regulator-side resolution machinery is statutory and bounded: the Monetary Authority 'may appoint a person to be a statutory manager of a licensee' (section 80), and the statutory manager 'may do anything necessary for the management of the licensee's affairs, business or property' subject to a primary objective the Monetary Authority specifies (section 86), while redemption-in-insolvency is pro rata against the segregated pool (Schedule 2 section 6(4)). The Guideline wires the trust to an orderly business exit plan (paragraph 6.8.17). Short of pass: a statutory manager could restructure the licensee, and the broad section 86 management power plus the redemption-delay lever (Guideline paragraph 6.8.21, delay requires the Monetary Authority's prior written consent) make officer reach less constrained than a pure pari-passu-pooling mandate. Refuter: the resolution power is bounded by the Ordinance and the section 80(3)(b) primary-objective constraint, but officer reach over the residual estate is wider than a fixed pari-passu pooling rule. Re-bound 2026-06-18 from the Explanatory Note to the Cap.656 primary statute (sections 80, 86) plus the Supervisory Guideline (paragraph 6.8.17).

regime: Hong Kong (Stablecoins Ordinance Cap. 656 / HKMA) [HKD FRS]
evidence_basis: No completed insolvency of a Cap. 656-licensed stablecoin issuer (the regime in force only since 2025-08-01); no in-class recovery record. Refuter: Hong Kong has adjacent crypto-insolvency practice but no SO-licensed-issuer-class record; graded fail in parallel with J11 and J14 as a new in-force regime. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE (CBUAE Payment Token Services Regulation, Circular 2/2024) [AED payment token]
evidence_basis: In force (effective 2024-07-06). Art 22.1.b requires the Reserve protected against creditor claims in all circumstances and Art 22.2 mandates a separate escrow account, but the regulation routes insolvency efficacy through Art 22.5's mandated effective CONTRACTUAL arrangement (with a Central-Bank-triggered external legal opinion to confirm legal soundness) and holds the escrow in the ISSUER'S OWN name (Art 22.2.b), not as a statutory estate carve-out (contrast J11 MiCA Art 36(2): creditors have no recourse to the reserve, in particular on insolvency) or a statutory trust (J13 HK 3.4.4; J7 Bermuda DABA s.18(4)). Refuter: the protection is regulatorily mandated but contractually delivered and held in the issuer's name, so its insolvency-effectiveness against UAE Decree-Law 51/2023 is contract-and-opinion-dependent, not self-executing statutory segregation; this parallels J9 Singapore D1 PARTIAL (strong custody statute, reserve side via licence conditions and trust structure) rather than the MiCA/HK statutory-carve-out PASS. Citation precision: cite Art 22.2/22.7 (not Art 22.1.b) for the escrow/segregation claim. Re-scored 2026-06-18 on adversarial verification of the jurisdiction-matrix expansion. D1 ladder: rung 4 (contractual; issuer-own-name escrow).

regime: UAE (CBUAE Payment Token Services Regulation, Circular 2/2024) [AED payment token]
evidence_basis: Art 22.5 grants holders a legal right and claim to redemption from the Reserve on the issuer's insolvency, and the Reserve is ringfenced from creditors (Art 22.1.b), fixing the holder's recovery ahead of general creditors. Refuter: the holder's residual rank in the UAE federal bankruptcy waterfall, beyond the Reserve, is not PTSR-fixed. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE (CBUAE Payment Token Services Regulation, Circular 2/2024) [AED payment token]
evidence_basis: Cross-border recognition is layered: ADGM (the Abu Dhabi common-law free zone) has adopted the UNCITRAL Model Law, while the federal onshore regime (Decree-Law 51/2023) has a narrower cross-border framework; the CBUAE PTSR is the federal-onshore regime. Refuter: onshore federal recognition is limited; the strong Model-Law recognition sits in the ADGM free-zone, a separate layer not yet acquired. Newly scored 2026-06-18 (regime-framework basis); one refuter.

regime: UAE (CBUAE Payment Token Services Regulation, Circular 2/2024) [AED payment token]
evidence_basis: The PTSR is the product-specific statutory regime, in force: reserve backing, escrow segregation, redemption rights, and licensing of payment-token issuance, conversion and custody. Refuter: the PTSR's interaction with the UAE federal bankruptcy law on distribution is untested. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE (CBUAE Payment Token Services Regulation, Circular 2/2024) [AED payment token]
evidence_basis: The Reserve is held in separate escrow and protected against creditor claims (Art 22.1.b), so the issuer cannot reuse or re-pledge it. Refuter: the escrow's insolvency-effectiveness and the precise re-pledge prohibition text are untested against the federal insolvency regime. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE (CBUAE Payment Token Services Regulation, Circular 2/2024) [AED payment token]
evidence_basis: The PTSR and the CBUAE supervisory intervention powers bound the resolution and holders redeem from the Reserve on insolvency (Art 22.5); but UAE federal insolvency law (Decree-Law 51/2023) governs the estate and could restructure a residual claim. Refuter: the redemption-from-Reserve right narrows officer reach but does not displace the federal insolvency regime. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE (CBUAE Payment Token Services Regulation, Circular 2/2024) [AED payment token]
evidence_basis: No completed insolvency of a CBUAE-licensed payment-token issuer (the PTSR in force only since 2024-07-06); no in-class recovery record. Refuter: no adjacent UAE crypto-issuer-class insolvency record; graded fail in parallel with J11 and J13 as a new in-force regime. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE Abu Dhabi free zone (ADGM FSRA virtual-asset framework; COBS Rulebook + Insolvency Regulations 2015) [comparative; custody-redemption-and-cross-border overlay]
cell_cite: COBS 14.12.1 (deemed trust), 14.13.1 / 14.13.2 / 14.13.5(a) (pooling-event distribution); COBS 15.3.2 / 15.4.1 (Safe-Custody segregation) read across to Virtual Assets via 17.1.3 + 17.8.1; ADGM Insolvency Regs 2015 S263(2) (client-asset rules prevail in insolvency)
evidence_basis: For the fiat (Client Money) leg, COBS 14.12.1 makes the firm a statutory trustee ("An Authorised Person receives and holds Client Money as trustee in accordance with the following trusts"), and on a Pooling Event clients are paid first from the segregated pool "in accordance with each Client's valid claim over that Money" (14.13.5(a)), with the Client Money Distribution Rules overriding inconsistent insolvency law (14.13.1). For the dominant Virtual-Asset leg, COBS 17.8.1 routes a VA custodian to Chapters 15/16, where Safe Custody Assets "must record, register and hold such Safe Custody Assets separately from its own Investments" (15.3.2) in a registered Client Account (15.4.1), and 17.1.3 reads "Investments" as "Virtual Assets," so the segregation mandate applies unchanged to custodied crypto. The Insolvency Regulations close the estate-exclusion loop: S263(2) provides that "The rules relating to client assets and client monies applicable to Authorised Persons shall not be regarded as invalid on the ground of inconsistency with these Regulations ... and those rules shall, to the extent of any inconsistency, prevail."

regime: UAE Abu Dhabi free zone (ADGM FSRA virtual-asset framework; COBS Rulebook + Insolvency Regulations 2015) [comparative; custody-redemption-and-cross-border overlay]
cell_cite: COBS 19A.4.1 (redeem at par on demand, T+2), 19A.7.1 (continuous full-reserve floor), 19A.11.1 (pro-rata reserve claim), 15.6.1(f) (holders' lien on reserve), 19A.11.2 (holder-priority duty), 19A.11.4 (super-priority waterfall) binding in formal insolvency via 19A.11.5, 19A.11.6 (residual shortfall unsecured)
evidence_basis: The holder has a hard, time-bound redemption right: the issuer must "redeem the Fiat-Referenced Tokens presented for redemption ... at par value" within T+2 (19A.4.1), collateralized by a continuously-tested floor under which reserve market value "is equal to or exceeds the total value of all outstanding redemption claims" (19A.7.1). On rank, holders "have a pro-rata claim against the Relevant Money and Reserve Investments" (19A.11.1), reserve assets at a third-party custodian are "subject to a lien in favour of the holders of the associated Fiat-Referenced Tokens" (15.6.1(f)), the issuer must ensure holder interests "rank in priority to interests of any other creditor of the Authorised Person" (19A.11.2), and the statutory waterfall pays "first, to all holders of all issued and outstanding Fiat-Referenced Tokens on a pro-rata basis" (19A.11.4(a)), a priority order that binds in a formal insolvency "in accordance with the Insolvency Regulations" (19A.11.5). Refuter: recovery is capped at the reserve pool, with any shortfall ranking only as an unsecured claim against the issuer (19A.11.6); PASS rests on the par-redemption-plus-senior-reserve-rank reading.

regime: UAE Abu Dhabi free zone (ADGM FSRA virtual-asset framework; COBS Rulebook + Insolvency Regulations 2015) [comparative; custody-redemption-and-cross-border overlay]
cell_cite: ADGM Insolvency Regs 2015 S271(1) (UNCITRAL Model Law force of law), S272(2) (Schedule 10 primacy); Schedule 10 Art 9 (direct access), Art 17(1) (mandatory recognition), Art 20 (automatic stay), Art 21(1)(e) (entrust ADGM assets to the foreign representative), Art 25 (cooperation duty)
evidence_basis: The ADGM has formally enacted, not merely referenced, the UNCITRAL Model Law on Cross-Border Insolvency: "The UNCITRAL Model Law shall have the force of law in the Abu Dhabi Global Market in the form set out in Schedule 10" (S271(1)), and on conflict with the general Regulations "the latter shall prevail" (S272(2)). The machinery is mandatory and complete: a foreign representative "is entitled to apply directly to the Court" (Art 9), "a foreign proceeding shall be recognised if" the documentary criteria are met (Art 17(1), subject only to the Art 6 public-policy exception), and on recognition the Court may grant relief "including ... entrusting the administration or realisation of all or part of the debtor's assets located in the Abu Dhabi Global Market to the foreign representative" (Art 21(1)(e)), with recognition of a foreign main proceeding triggering an automatic stay "the same in scope and effect as if the debtor had been made the subject of a winding-up order" (Art 20). Refuter: the PASS rests on enacted-statute strength (force-of-law Model Law, mandatory recognition); unlike J1 (Morning Mist) and J9 (Zipmex / Quoine), ADGM has no demonstrated cross-border insolvency practice, so the recognition machinery is untested in an ADGM crypto-issuer case. The statute is in force and recognition mandatory, so PASS holds on the primary text.

regime: UAE Abu Dhabi free zone (ADGM FSRA virtual-asset framework; COBS Rulebook + Insolvency Regulations 2015) [comparative; custody-redemption-and-cross-border overlay]
cell_cite: COBS Ch 19A: 19A.2 (white paper), 19A.3 (activity ring-fence), 19A.4 (redemption at par), 19A.5 (reserve composition/custody), 19A.6 (per-token segregation), 19A.7 (daily-valued full backing), 19A.8 (stress testing), 19A.9 / 19A.10 (monthly attestation + annual external audit), 19A.11 (wind-down waterfall); on the Ch 17 VA accepted-asset / custody backbone
evidence_basis: ADGM Chapter 19A is a comprehensive product-specific FRT regime layered on the Chapter 17 VA backbone (17.1.3 reads "Investments" in the Safe-Custody rules as "Virtual Assets"). It mandates per-token reserve ring-fencing (the issuer "must segregate all Reserve Assets for each Fiat-Referenced Token from every other Fiat-Referenced Token," 19A.6.1), a continuous full-backing floor with daily valuation (19A.7.1-19A.7.2), an at-least-annual independent external audit of reserves and controls ("perform an annual audit of," 19A.10.1) on top of monthly attestation (19A.9), a mandatory pre-issuance white paper disclosing "redemption rights at par, rights to interest or other time-based payments" (19A.2.1), and an activity ring-fence under which the issuer "is prohibited from carrying on any additional Regulated Activities not incidental to the issuance of a Fiat-Referenced Token" (19A.3.1).

regime: UAE Abu Dhabi free zone (ADGM FSRA virtual-asset framework; COBS Rulebook + Insolvency Regulations 2015) [comparative; custody-redemption-and-cross-border overlay]
cell_cite: COBS 15.4.4 ('must not use ... without that Client's prior written permission'), 15.4.5 (permitted-use-with-consent framework), read across to Virtual Assets via 17.1.3 + 17.8.1; reserve side: COBS 15.4.6 (absolute 'in any circumstances' prohibition on Reserve Investments)
evidence_basis: COBS 15.4.4 is a binding no-use rule: "An Authorised Person must not use a Client's Safe Custody Assets for its own purpose or that of another Person without that Client's prior written permission," and 15.4.5 supplies the permitted-use framework requiring that "the equivalent assets are returned to the Client Account of the Client" and that "the Client is not disadvantaged by the use of his Safe Custody Assets." Chapter 17 carries this unchanged to Accepted Virtual Assets (17.8.1 routes VA custody to "the requirements set out in COBS, Chapters 15 and 16," and 17.1.3 reads "Investments" as "Virtual Assets," with no VA-specific reuse override). This is the per-use prior-written-consent structure the adopted D5 ladder grades PASS at J3 (23 NYCRR 200.9(c), "except at that person's direction") and J9 (Singapore reg 18C(1)(c), "except on the customer's specific prior written instructions"), and it is distinguishable from the Cayman PARTIAL (disclosure-conditioned consent). For the reserve side specifically, COBS 15.4.6 imposes an absolute prohibition: an issuer "must not use Reserve Investments for its own purposes in any circumstances." Refuter: COBS Chapter 15 has no absolute consent-independent no-rehypothecation rule for general client custody assets; the per-use prior-written-consent form grades PASS under the adopted D5 ladder (parallel to J3 / J9), and the absolute prohibition applies to Reserve Investments (15.4.6).

regime: UAE Abu Dhabi free zone (ADGM FSRA virtual-asset framework; COBS Rulebook + Insolvency Regulations 2015) [comparative; custody-redemption-and-cross-border overlay]
cell_cite: ADGM Insolvency Regs 2015 S1(1) (administrator manages the estate), S4 (officer of the court), S95 (general + director-removal + management-freeze powers), S97(1) (custody-of-all-property duty), Schedule 2 paras 1/2/12 (possess/sell/realise), S44 / S45 (moratorium); parallel receivership track S152 + Schedule 3
evidence_basis: The administrator is appointed "to manage the Company's affairs, business and property" (S1(1)) as "an officer of the Court" (S4), "may do anything necessary or expedient for the management of the affairs, business and property of the Company" (S95(1)), may remove and appoint directors (S95(5)) while the company "may not exercise a management power without the consent of the administrator" (S95(8)), and "shall on his appointment take custody or control of all the property to which he thinks the Company is entitled" (S97(1)), backed by the Schedule 2 power to "take possession of, collect and get in the property of the Company" and to "sell or otherwise dispose of the property of the Company." A statutory moratorium bars creditor self-help: "No legal process (including legal proceedings) may be instituted or continued against the Company or property of the Company except ... with the consent of the administrator ... or ... the permission of the Court" (S45(5)), with security enforcement similarly stayed (S45(2)).

regime: UAE Abu Dhabi free zone (ADGM FSRA virtual-asset framework; COBS Rulebook + Insolvency Regulations 2015) [comparative; custody-redemption-and-cross-border overlay]
cell_cite: no ADGM FRT-issuer / VA-custodian insolvency precedent; Insolvency Regs 2015 are product-class-neutral (one generic non-crypto bridge at S263(2))
evidence_basis: A full-text search of the Insolvency Regulations returns no virtual-asset, crypto, stablecoin, fiat-referenced-token, or issuer-specific insolvency provision and no decided in-class case; the regime is product-class-neutral, and the FRT framework is new in force. The single adjacent touchpoint is generic, not crypto-specific: S263(2) provides that the COBS "rules relating to client assets and client monies applicable to Authorised Persons ... shall, to the extent of any inconsistency, prevail" in an administration, Deed of Company Arrangement, or winding-up.

regime: UAE Dubai free zone (VARA binding Rulebooks) [comparative; estate-exclusion]
evidence_basis: Binding VARA Rulebook estate-exclusion, in force: custodied virtual assets are 'not depository liabilities or assets of the VASP' (Custody Services Rulebook III.B.1), each client's assets sit in 'separate VA Wallets containing the Virtual Assets of that client only' (III.B.3), and FRVA reserves are held so they are 'legally segregated and remote from their own assets' and 'do not form a part of their estate' (FRVA Rules III.B.4(a)). On the D1 mechanism ladder this is binding-regulatory estate-exclusion (rung 1, the MiCA/Bermuda/Swiss rung), materially stronger than the federal J14 D1 PARTIAL (issuer-name contractual escrow, rung 4). Refuter: the exclusion binds via property law (the client owns the VAs, the VASP is custodian) plus VARA mandatory segregation rather than a federal bankruptcy-code carve-out, a VARA VASP insolvency is administered in the UAE federal onshore forum, and the basis is untested in that forum for virtual assets, so it is a candidate PASS carrying the property-law-plus-regulatory-segregation qualifier, not a self-executing federal statutory carve-out. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE Dubai free zone (VARA binding Rulebooks) [comparative; estate-exclusion]
evidence_basis: The binding FRVA reserve rule fixes the holder's recovery ahead of the issuer's general creditors: reserves 'will not otherwise be subject to any recourse by their creditors, the custodian of the Reserve Assets or any other third parties, in particular in the event that they become Insolvent' (FRVA Rules III.B.4(c)), and holders carry a 'valid legally enforceable right to redeem the FRVA at par' (FRVA Rules III.C.1), so the ringfenced reserve satisfies holders before creditors reach it. Custodied client VAs are likewise outside the VASP estate (Custody III.B.1). Refuter: a residual shortfall claim beyond the segregated reserve ranks under the UAE federal onshore insolvency waterfall, which the VARA Rulebooks do not rank; parallel to the J11/J13/J14 D2 pattern (segregated-reserve recovery priority-fixed, residual rank national or federal law). Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE Dubai free zone (VARA binding Rulebooks) [comparative; estate-exclusion]
cell_cite: UAE federal onshore forum; ADGM free-zone layer scored separately
evidence_basis: Cross-border posture is scored on the UAE federal onshore forum because VARA covers Dubai mainland, so a VARA VASP insolvency is administered onshore at the federal level rather than in a separate free-zone forum: the federal onshore regime has a narrower cross-border framework, while the stronger cross-border recognition sits in the ADGM common-law free zone, a separately scored free-zone layer. Mirrors the federal J14 D3 PARTIAL. Refuter: onshore federal recognition is limited and the robust recognition lives in the ADGM layer, not in the forum that would administer a VARA VASP insolvency, and no Dubai VA-issuer cross-border recognition track record exists. Newly scored 2026-06-18 (regime-framework basis); one refuter.

regime: UAE Dubai free zone (VARA binding Rulebooks) [comparative; estate-exclusion]
cell_cite: Custody III.B; FRVA III.B/III.C; Company Rulebook Wind Down item k
evidence_basis: A comprehensive product-specific binding-Rulebook layer is in force: custody segregation and estate-exclusion (Custody Services Rulebook III.B), FRVA reserve segregation and redemption rules (FRVA Rules III.B/III.C), and a mandatory Wind Down Plan that must 'ensure that the sale of Client Money and/or Client VAs is explicitly excluded from, and not necessary for, implementing or completing the Wind Down Plan' (Company Rulebook Part V Wind Down Plan item k), with VARA holding intervention and assume-control power over Client Money and Client VAs. Parallel to J11/J13/J15 D4 PASS (comprehensive in-force product-specific instrument). Refuter: the binding Rulebooks' interaction with UAE federal onshore bankruptcy distribution is untested for virtual assets, and the operative Client Virtual Assets Rules cross-referenced from Custody Part V sit in a separate VARA Compliance and Risk Management Rulebook not in this extract. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE Dubai free zone (VARA binding Rulebooks) [comparative; estate-exclusion]
evidence_basis: Absolute binding no-rehypothecation prohibition with no consent exception: VASPs 'shall not authorise or permit rehypothecation of Virtual Assets for which they provide Custody Services' (Custody Services Rulebook III.B.2), a prohibition that applies regardless of client consent and that the rule also bars the VASP from seeking, and the FRVA rule requires reserves not be 'rehypothecated, or subject to any pledges, encumbrances, right of set-off or counterclaim' (FRVA Rules III.B.4(b)). On the substance-based D5 ladder (ruling Q5) this is the strongest rung: a binding prohibition with no consent carve-out at all, exceeding the customer-consent-preserved PASSes at J3 and J9. Refuter: the Custody no-rehypothecation rule binds the VASP as custodian, while FRVA reserves are a distinct asset class ('Reserve Assets are not Client Money or Client VAs'), so the prohibition reaches the reserve through III.B.4(b) rather than III.B.2; both are binding-Rulebook prohibitions, so PASS stands. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE Dubai free zone (VARA binding Rulebooks) [comparative; estate-exclusion]
cell_cite: FRVA III.C.1; Company Rulebook Wind Down item k
evidence_basis: The binding redeem-at-par right ('valid legally enforceable right to redeem the FRVA at par', FRVA Rules III.C.1) plus the Wind Down Plan exclusion of any Client Money or Client VA sale (Company Rulebook Part V item k) bound the wind-down and keep client assets beyond the liquidator's sale powers, and FRVA holders are disclosed that the FRVA is 'not covered by any investor protection or deposit guarantee schemes'. But the estate proceeding runs in the UAE federal onshore forum, which governs the residual estate and could restructure a residual claim, and VARA's own direction power over reserves operates without holder consent. Refuter: the redeem-at-par and wind-down-exclusion discipline narrows officer reach but does not displace the federal onshore insolvency regime over a residual claim; parallel to J11/J13/J14 D6 PARTIAL. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: UAE Dubai free zone (VARA binding Rulebooks) [comparative; estate-exclusion]
cell_cite: VARA regime in force 2023; no in-class insolvency
evidence_basis: No completed insolvency of a VARA-licensed VASP or FRVA issuer has tested the estate-exclusion and segregation Rulebook provisions; the regime, issued under the Virtual Assets and Related Activities Regulations 2023, is new in force and the binding Rulebook segregation has no in-class recovery record. Refuter: the UAE has adjacent commercial-insolvency practice and VARA holds intervention and assume-control powers over Client Money and Client VAs, but there is no Dubai VA-issuer or custodian-class completed insolvency demonstrating the segregation rule in operation; graded FAIL on the in-class basis the dimension scores, in parallel with J11/J13/J14/J15 D7 as a new in-force regime. Newly scored 2026-06-18; one refuter.

regime: Switzerland (DLT Act 2021; DEBA Art 242a; CO 973d-973i; FinMIA 73a-73f) [comparative]
evidence_basis: Statutory estate carve-out, in force 2021-08-01: DEBA Art 242a orders surrender of crypto-based assets from the bankruptcy estate where the debtor undertook to hold them in readiness at all times and they are allocated individually to the third party or to community ownership with a clearly identifiable share (source NOTES KC-3, sidecar lines 227-237); Banking Act Art 16(1bis)/37d give banks and securities firms a parallel custody-asset segregation with liquidator exclusion from the estate (KC-4, lines 385-390). Structurally parallel to J7 Bermuda D1 (DABA s.18(4) 'not subject to the claims of creditors') and J11 MiCA D1 (Art 36(2) reserve segregated from the estate). Refuter: the carve-out is conditional, not automatic; collective custody without clearly identifiable shares drops into the estate and the holder becomes an unsecured creditor (WebSearch: FINMA Guidance 01/2026; CMS / Borel Barbey), but the qualifying-custody case (the matrix's scored anchor for a regulated Swiss custodian) is a clean statutory segregation, so PASS on the same basis the matrix grades J7 and J11. D1 ladder: rung 1 (statutory estate-exclusion).

regime: Switzerland (DLT Act 2021; DEBA Art 242a; CO 973d-973i; FinMIA 73a-73f) [comparative]
cell_cite: DEBA Art 242a surrender; no crypto-holder priority class
evidence_basis: Qualifying-custody holders recover the segregated assets outside the estate by surrender order (DEBA Art 242a; property, not priority), fixing recovery ahead of the custodian's general creditors. But no Swiss statute fixes a stablecoin/tokenised-asset holder's rank in the bankruptcy waterfall: a residual shortfall claim, or any non-qualifying holder, ranks as an ordinary unsecured creditor under general Swiss insolvency law, and locating that rank requires the same multi-step characterization the rubric names as PARTIAL. Parallel to J7 D2, J9 D2, and J11/J13/J14 D2 (segregated reserve recovery fixed; residual rank national/general law). Refuter: one could read the Art 242a surrender right as functionally priority-grade for the holder, but it is an estate-exclusion mechanism, not a waterfall-rank rule, and the shortfall leg is unranked, so PARTIAL is the supported grade.

regime: Switzerland (DLT Act 2021; DEBA Art 242a; CO 973d-973i; FinMIA 73a-73f) [comparative]
cell_cite: PILA Art 166 et seq. (2019 reform); no Model Law
