evidence_basis: Switzerland has not adopted the UNCITRAL Model Law but operates its own statutory cross-border insolvency recognition regime under PILA Chapter 11 (Art 166 et seq.), substantially modernized effective 2019-01-01: recognition extended to the debtor's COMI (not only domicile), the reciprocity requirement abolished, secondary Swiss proceedings dispensable where no privileged claims exist, and Swiss authorities authorized to coordinate with foreign counterparts (WebSearch: VISCHER; MLL News Portal; Chambers Insolvency 2022 Switzerland). Recognition is by application/exequatur and discretionary in mechanics rather than automatic and uniform, and there is no tokenised-asset-issuer recognition track record. Graded PARTIAL consistent with the matrix treatment of robust statutory-but-non-uniform recognition regimes (J6 Cayman Part 17 inbound PARTIAL; J11 MiCA intra-EU EIR PARTIAL), and materially stronger than the common-law-comity-only PARTIALs (J7, J13). Refuter: the 2019 reform is modern and robust enough to argue PASS by analogy to J9 (IRDA Model Law) or J12 (CBIR 2006); held to PARTIAL because the regime is recognition-on-application without the automatic-recognition uniformity of a Model Law enactment and carries no in-class crypto precedent. Primary-text re-verification of PILA Art 166-175 flagged.

regime: Switzerland (DLT Act 2021; DEBA Art 242a; CO 973d-973i; FinMIA 73a-73f) [comparative]
evidence_basis: A genuine product-specific statutory layer for tokenised assets is in force: the Code of Obligations ledger-based-security framework (Art 973d-973i) creates a statutory on-ledger security instrument with take-free protection and certificated-security-equivalent insolvency treatment (source NOTES KC-1, KC-2), and FinMIA Art 73a-73f create the DLT trading facility licence, a combined trading/custody/clearing/settlement authorization for DLT securities with Federal-Council-set additional requirements modeled on CSD rules (KC-5; WebSearch: FINMA 2021 SDX/SDX Trading approvals; sif.admin.ch). Parallel to J11/J13/J14 D4 PASS (comprehensive in-force product-specific statute). Refuter: the DLT-trading-facility additional requirements are Federal-Council-delegated and the first such facility was authorized only in 2025 (WebSearch: Global Legal Insights 2026; FINMA), so the venue layer is thinly operationalized; but the in-force CO ledger-based-security statute plus the Banking Act custody regime supply the product-class statutory layer this dimension scores, so PASS.

regime: Switzerland (DLT Act 2021; DEBA Art 242a; CO 973d-973i; FinMIA 73a-73f) [comparative]
evidence_basis: Substance-based D5-ladder PASS: a Swiss bank/securities-firm custodian must hold qualifying crypto-based assets as segregated custody assets excluded from its estate (Banking Act Art 16(1bis)/37d), and FINMA may cap the volume of crypto custody assets a bank holds based on technology-specific risks (Art 4sexies; source NOTES KC-4, sidecar lines 378-382). The segregation-plus-custody architecture means the custodian cannot reuse or re-pledge the assets for its own account, the same mechanism the matrix grades PASS for J11 MiCA D5 (segregation Art 36 plus custody Art 37-38, no single express no-rehypothecation article) and J13/J14. Refuter: Swiss law has no single express no-rehypothecation/no-reuse article in this statute analogous to 23 NYCRR 200.9, and the FINMA volume-cap is discretionary case-by-case rather than a hard quantitative prohibition; but under the adopted substance-based ladder (ruling Q5) the segregation-plus-custody-mandate lattice yields PASS on the same basis as J11.

regime: Switzerland (DLT Act 2021; DEBA Art 242a; CO 973d-973i; FinMIA 73a-73f) [comparative]
cell_cite: Banking Act / FINMA resolution; DEBA not displaced
evidence_basis: Segregated crypto-based assets sit outside the estate (DEBA Art 242a; Banking Act Art 37d), bounding the insolvency officer's reach over qualifying-custody assets. But the federal bank-insolvency and FINMA recovery-and-resolution regime (and general DEBA insolvency law for non-bank issuers) is not displaced and supplies restructuring/resolution powers a FINMA-appointed administrator could exercise over the residual estate and residual holder claims. Parallel to J11/J13/J14 D6 PARTIAL (product-specific redemption discipline narrows officer reach but national/federal insolvency law is not displaced) and J9 D6. Refuter: one could argue PASS because the Art 242a estate-exclusion places the core asset beyond officer reach; held to PARTIAL because the residual claim and any non-qualifying custody remain inside a resolution regime with restructuring powers that are not pre-agreed product triggers.

regime: Switzerland (DLT Act 2021; DEBA Art 242a; CO 973d-973i; FinMIA 73a-73f) [comparative]
evidence_basis: No completed insolvency of a Swiss tokenised-asset issuer or regulated crypto custodian has tested DEBA Art 242a segregation; no Swiss court decision applying Art 242a to a crypto insolvency surfaced (WebSearch: no precedent in the FINMA Guidance 01/2026 coverage, Lexology, Mondaq, or the cross-border-insolvency sources). The named Swiss crypto banks (Sygnum, AMINA/formerly SEBA, Bank Frick) are going concerns; FlowBank SA (FINMA-mandated bankruptcy effective 2024-06-13) was a Geneva brokerage/trading bank, not a tokenised-asset issuer, and produced no Art 242a segregation precedent; the first DLT trading facility was authorized only in 2025 (WebSearch: Finance Magnates; Global Legal Insights 2026; FINMA). Graded FAIL in parallel with J11/J13/J14 D7 (new in-force regime, no in-class recovery record). Refuter: Switzerland has adjacent bank-insolvency practice and the 2019 PILA regime has general cross-border recovery experience, but there is no crypto-issuer/custodian-class completed insolvency demonstrating the segregation rule in operation, so FAIL on the in-class basis the dimension scores.

regime: Japan PSA electronic-payment-instrument framework (specified-trust-beneficiary-rights + performance-guarantee deposit; FSA 2025 implementing instruments) [comparative; trust-and-deposit backing]
cell_cite: PSA Art 2(9) trust scheme + EPI ord Art 3(1) 50% deposit floor; perf-guarantee deposit duty PSA Art 43 (Art 44/45 substitutes); Art 62-14
evidence_basis: Statutory segregation via two mechanisms under the Payment Services Act electronic-payment-instrument (EPI) regime (2022 Act No. 61, implemented by the FSA 2025 instruments, in force June 2026). Trust route: specified trust beneficiary rights (PSA Art 2(9); the Electronic Payment Instrument III scheme), under which the customer holds the beneficiary right and the trust property is the beneficiary's and bankruptcy-remote from the issuer estate; the 2025 EPI ordinance Art 3(1) requires at least 50 percent of the trust property to be held in bank deposits, the remainder restricted to short-maturity Japanese government bonds and high-grade foreign sovereign bonds (the 2025 trust-beneficiary-bond standards notice sets the foreign-bond standard). Deposit route: a performance-guarantee deposit, the duty under PSA Art 43, escrowed with the Legal Affairs Bureau, with the preservation contract (Art 44) and the trust contract (Art 45) as substitutes. Intermediary-level segregation plus mandatory CPA audit (PSA Art 62-14). Reconciled across the 2025 Japanese primaries, the Anderson Mori 2022 bulletin (which describes the 2022 entire-in-bank-deposits rule the 2025 ordinance relaxed to the 50 percent floor; customers' redemption claims are 'properly protected in the event of the default of issuers or intermediaries'), and 2025 law-firm guides. D1 ladder rung 1 to 2 (trust estate-exclusion via beneficiary rights, or statutory deposit-escrow). Refuter: the trust backing is a 50 percent deposit floor plus a restricted high-grade sleeve rather than full deposits, but the segregation and bankruptcy-remoteness of the trust property are statutory either way, so PASS holds on the statutory-segregation basis the dimension scores.

regime: Japan PSA electronic-payment-instrument framework (specified-trust-beneficiary-rights + performance-guarantee deposit; FSA 2025 implementing instruments) [comparative; trust-and-deposit backing]
cell_cite: PSA Art 59(1) priority over other creditors; perf-guarantee regs Art 7 (table) + Art 10 (distribution)
evidence_basis: Statutory user priority. Under the trust route, the customer's specified trust beneficiary right is a direct claim on the segregated trust property, ranking ahead of the issuer's general creditors. Under the deposit route, holders have a statutory right to be paid from the performance-guarantee deposit ahead of other creditors (PSA Art 59(1)), implemented through an FSA-administered process: a provisional distribution table after the claim-investigation period (performance-guarantee-deposit regs Art 7) and then the distribution (Art 10). Anderson Mori 2022: customers' redemption claims are 'clearly secured' and 'properly protected in the event of the default of issuers or intermediaries.' Statutory and contract-independent. Refuter: a funds-transfer-type holder's rank turns on the deposit-distribution machinery rather than a bankruptcy-code priority class, but PSA Art 59(1) fixes the user-first ordering, so PASS, parallel to J13 and J14Z D2.

regime: Japan PSA electronic-payment-instrument framework (specified-trust-beneficiary-rights + performance-guarantee deposit; FSA 2025 implementing instruments) [comparative; trust-and-deposit backing]
evidence_basis: Japan adopted the UNCITRAL Model Law via the Act on Recognition and Assistance for Foreign Insolvency Proceedings (Act No. 129 of 2000, in force 2001, among the earliest adopters), but the adoption is materially modified from the original: the recognition order has no effect in itself and operates only as a precondition for a separate assistance order (stay or repayment-prohibition), and several Model Law provisions were not adopted (UNCITRAL status list; Norton Rose and Mori Hamada cross-border-insolvency surveys). No verified in-class crypto-issuer cross-border recognition precedent surfaced for the EPI regime; the largest Japanese crypto insolvency (Mt. Gox) is an exchange civil rehabilitation predating the EPI framework. Graded PARTIAL: Model Law present but modified, and no in-class practice, weaker than J9 Singapore D3 (force-of-law IRDA s.252 plus Zipmex and Quoine) and parallel to the J13/J15 partial-recognition posture. Refuter: Japan's Model Law adoption is real and decades-tested for general insolvencies, but the modifications plus the absence of in-class crypto-issuer practice keep it below a clean PASS.

regime: Japan PSA electronic-payment-instrument framework (specified-trust-beneficiary-rights + performance-guarantee deposit; FSA 2025 implementing instruments) [comparative; trust-and-deposit backing]
evidence_basis: A comprehensive product-class statutory regime, not a structural workaround: the Payment Services Act defines electronic payment instruments (Art 2(5)), the specified-trust-beneficiary-rights scheme (Art 2(9)), the closed issuer set (specified trust companies and trust banks, banks, and licensed funds-transfer providers; Art 2(27) to (28), 37-2), redemption at par, the backing and segregation duties, and the intermediary code of conduct with segregation and CPA audit (Art 62-12 to 62-15), all implemented in detail by the FSA 2025 cabinet order and ordinances. Strongest-tier product-specific statutory layer, parallel to J7 Bermuda DABA and J13 Hong Kong Cap. 656. Refuter: the regime is new and its provisions are read in translation, but the product-specific statutory scope is confirmed across the primaries and the Anderson Mori secondary, so PASS.

regime: Japan PSA electronic-payment-instrument framework (specified-trust-beneficiary-rights + performance-guarantee deposit; FSA 2025 implementing instruments) [comparative; trust-and-deposit backing]
cell_cite: EPI ord Art 3(1) 50% deposit floor + restricted JGB/sovereign sleeve; deposit escrowed, no rehypothecation
evidence_basis: A binding reserve-use constraint above contract. The specified-trust-beneficiary-rights backing must be held with at least 50 percent in bank deposits (2025 EPI ordinance Art 3(1)), the remainder restricted to short-maturity Japanese government bonds and high-grade foreign sovereign bonds (the 2025 trust-beneficiary-bond standards notice limits the foreign-bond portion to sovereign bonds whose debtor credit rating is in the highest categories, credit-risk category 1-2 or better at every qualified rating agency, with national issuance exceeding the equivalent of 100 trillion yen). The performance-guarantee deposit is escrowed and cannot be deployed as the issuer's own balance-sheet asset. The trustee cannot freely re-pledge or re-use the trust property. Statutory or ordinance-level constraint, not a disclosure-conditioned carve-out; PASS under the adopted D5 ladder, parallel to J13/J14Z/J15. Refuter: the deposit floor is 50 percent rather than full, but the non-deposit remainder is itself restricted to short-maturity high-grade sovereigns and the backing cannot be rehypothecated, so PASS.

regime: Japan PSA electronic-payment-instrument framework (specified-trust-beneficiary-rights + performance-guarantee deposit; FSA 2025 implementing instruments) [comparative; trust-and-deposit backing]
evidence_basis: Distribution is statutorily bounded and in money form: on issuer failure the FSA prepares and publishes a provisional distribution table after the claim-investigation period and then distributes the performance-guarantee deposit, the trust property is reachable directly by beneficiaries, and redemption is at par. Short of pass because the issuer's failure resolves within the general bankruptcy, civil-rehabilitation, or corporate-reorganization framework, which can restructure issuer-level claims and affect timing, and the EPI distribution machinery has no completed-case clock. PARTIAL, parallel to J14Z and J11/J13 D6. Refuter: the user-protective distribution is statutory and bounded, but the rehabilitation and reorganization overlay plus the absence of a tested timeline keep it at partial rather than pass.

regime: Japan PSA electronic-payment-instrument framework (specified-trust-beneficiary-rights + performance-guarantee deposit; FSA 2025 implementing instruments) [comparative; trust-and-deposit backing]
cell_cite: no in-class EPI-issuer insolvency; new regime; Mt. Gox adjacent
evidence_basis: No completed insolvency of a Japanese electronic-payment-instrument (stablecoin) issuer under the EPI regime; the framework is new (2022 Act, 2025 implementing instruments in force June 2026) and no in-class issuer has failed. The largest Japanese crypto insolvency, Mt. Gox (bankruptcy 2014, converted to civil rehabilitation 2018), is adjacent only: it is an exchange, not an EPI issuer, and predates the regime, so it tests no part of the trust-beneficiary or performance-guarantee machinery. Graded FAIL in parallel with J11/J13/J14Z/J15 D7 (new in-force regime, no in-class recovery record); the product verdict caps at PARTIAL per ruling Q1a. Refuter: Japan has deep general-insolvency and adjacent crypto-exchange (Mt. Gox) practice, but no in-class EPI-issuer completed insolvency demonstrates the segregation and distribution rules in operation, so FAIL on the in-class basis the dimension scores.
